gm.name: Jon-Eric Sullivan
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Vice President of Player Personnel, Green Bay Packers

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ted Thompson and Brian Gutekunst (Green Bay Packers). Sullivan explicitly cited Thompson in his Miami introductory presser as a key influence, and worked alongside Gutekunst for 22 years.
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Ron Wolf → Ted Thompson → Brian Gutekunst → Jon-Eric Sullivan. This is one of the most storied scouting trees in the NFL, producing John Schneider (SEA), Scot McCloughan, Reggie McKenzie, John Dorsey, and Gutekunst himself. The core DNA: build through the draft, retain your own players, supplement with targeted free agency, value elite athleticism and size, prioritize culture and character, be willing to draft QBs even when you have a starter. Sullivan is a direct product of this lineage, having spent his entire 22-year NFL career in Green Bay.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: No known breakpoints yet — Sullivan has zero drafts of his own. His stated philosophy in press conferences is perfectly aligned with the Packers model. One potential departure: Sullivan has signaled strong willingness to trade DOWN in the 2026 draft to accumulate picks, which aligns with classic Thompson-era philosophy but may differ from Gutekunst's more recent willingness to stay put or trade up. Sullivan has also stated he wants to build 'inside out' — prioritizing OL and DL — which echoes the Packers but may represent a stronger emphasis than GB typically displayed.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Zero drafts as a GM. Lineage weight is extremely high. Sullivan spent 22 years in one organization and has directly stated he is bringing the Green Bay philosophy to Miami. His public statements at the combine and introductory presser are perfectly consistent with the Packers model. The 0.85 weight captures the small possibility he diverges from the tree in practice. Will drop to 0.60 after 2026 draft and to 0.40 after 2027 draft.

key_influencers.name: Jeff Hafley
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Defensive-minded coach (DB coaching background). Favors 4-3 base defense, press-man coverage, single-high safety looks, vision-based zone concepts. Will heavily influence defensive personnel priorities — particularly CB, EDGE, DT, and S archetypes. Previously worked with Sullivan in Green Bay (2024-2025). Coached DBs in Tampa Bay, Cleveland, and San Francisco before head coaching at Boston College (2020-2023). His scheme demands specific player types: long, physical press corners; penetrating DTs; range safeties; athletic LBs who can cover.

key_influencers.name: Kyle Smith
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Son of former Chargers GM A.J. Smith. Spent 11 years in Washington's front office (2010-2020) rising from intern to VP of Player Personnel, then 5 years with the Falcons as VP of Player Personnel / Assistant GM. Strong scouting background with two different organizational philosophies. Played a key role in the 2025 Falcons draft. His Washington/Falcons background provides a different perspective from the Packers model Sullivan and Hafley bring.

key_influencers.name: Jon Robinson
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Executive
key_influencers.known_biases: Former GM of the Tennessee Titans (2016-2022). New England Patriots scouting tree (area scout in 2002, Director of College Scouting by 2009). Also spent time with Tampa Bay as Director of Player Personnel (2013). Has a track record of drafting physical, tough players. His Patriots/Titans background adds another layer to the front office. Robinson drafted Derrick Henry, A.J. Brown, Jeffery Simmons, and Harold Landry. Known for building physical rosters.

key_influencers.name: Champ Kelly
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Executive
key_influencers.known_biases: Background includes Denver Broncos scouting (2007-2014, part of the Super Bowl 50 roster construction), Chicago Bears (2015-2021), Las Vegas Raiders (assistant GM), and then Miami as interim GM in late 2025. Experienced in scouting department revamps. Adds a diverse organizational perspective.

key_influencers.name: Stephen Ross
key_influencers.title: Owner / Chairman
key_influencers.known_biases: Historically an active owner who has made coaching decisions and has preferences about team direction. Ross fired Mike McDaniel and Chris Grier, and was 'sold on Sullivan's vision' within 10 minutes of their first virtual interview. Has stated desire for 'comprehensive change' and 'championship-caliber team.' Ross is loyal to his hires once committed. He hired Sullivan and allowed him to pick Hafley, suggesting he's giving Sullivan real authority.

front_office.power_dynamics: Sullivan appears to have full control over draft and personnel decisions. Ross gave Sullivan the authority to hire his own head coach (Hafley), clean house in the front office (firing assistant GM Marvin Allen, co-directors of player personnel Hunt and Engroff), and build his own staff. Sullivan and Hafley were paired from Green Bay and share a common vision. The coaching and scouting staffs are almost entirely new. This is Sullivan's operation, with Hafley as his collaborative partner on the defensive side. Bobby Slowik (OC, retained from 2025 as pass game coordinator) provides offensive continuity.
front_office.owner_interference: medium — Ross is historically involved and opinionated but appears to have given Sullivan a genuine mandate for 'comprehensive change.' He was hands-on in the GM search (hired Troy Aikman as a consultant) but seems committed to letting Sullivan operate. Risk of interference remains if the rebuild takes longer than expected.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Build through the draft, develop and retain. BPA with a 'team-value approach' that subconsciously stacks for need. Build from the inside-out (OL/DL first). Culture-driven drafting.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: No personal draft history yet. Based entirely on stated philosophy and Packers lineage. Sullivan explicitly described a BPA approach with a subconscious need overlay: 'As we build a board, subconsciously, with a team-value approach, you'll stack it for need, if that makes sense. In free agency, we'll be much more intentional about where we need to fill gaps.' The Packers under Thompson/Gutekunst have historically leaned BPA in round 1 but filled needs more aggressively in rounds 2-3. The 0.70 BPA weight reflects this — moderately BPA-leaning overall with need-filling via free agency.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: No draft picks yet to analyze mismatch. The Packers under Gutekunst have occasionally reached for need (Quay Walker at LB in 2022, Jordan Love at QB in 2020 as a future investment). Sullivan may show similar tendencies. Monitor closely in the 2026 draft.

trade_triggers: Franchise quarterback opportunity (Sullivan explicitly stated the Packers' willingness to draft QBs proactively even when not needed — see Rodgers behind Favre, Love behind Rodgers)
Clear tier break where next tier of players is significantly below current available talent
Accumulating Day 2 and Day 3 picks for a roster in rebuild mode
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Sullivan has explicitly stated he wants to trade DOWN in 2026 to acquire more picks, not trade up. He said at the combine: 'We need more picks.' The Dolphins hold 5 top-100 picks but Sullivan has signaled he wants to grow that number. This aligns with the Thompson-era Packers philosophy of accumulating picks through trades. Sullivan has also signaled openness to trading current roster players (Minkah Fitzpatrick, possibly Tua Tagovailoa) for additional draft capital. Grier's prior regime frequently traded picks for established players; Sullivan represents a 180-degree reversal.

position_avoidance: RB in round 1 — The Packers under Thompson/Gutekunst have never used a 1st-round pick on a running back. Aaron Jones was a 5th-round pick, Eddie Lacy a 2nd-rounder, AJ Dillon a 2nd-rounder. Sullivan is unlikely to break this pattern, especially with De'Von Achane already on the roster.
position_premium: OL — Sullivan explicitly stated 'we've got to be dominant on the offensive line.' The Packers have consistently invested premium capital in OL: Elgton Jenkins (R2, 2019), Josh Myers (R2, 2021), Zach Tom (R4, 2022 — became a starter), Sean Rhyan (R3, 2022), Jordan Morgan (R1, 2024), Anthony Belton (R2, 2025).

DL — The Packers have drafted DL early repeatedly: Kenny Clark (R1, 2016), Rashan Gary (R1, 2019), Devonte Wyatt (R1, 2022), Lukas Van Ness (R1, 2023). Sullivan will prioritize both EDGE and interior DL.

QB — Sullivan explicitly stated: 'The quarterback position is the most important position in sports. We're going to invest in that position every year if we can.' He confirmed at the combine that the Dolphins WILL draft a QB in 2026.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Big-bodied, highly athletic EDGE rusher with room for growth. The Packers' typical M.O. under Gutekunst (with Sullivan) was drafting players like Rashan Gary (6-4, 277, 4.58, top-5 RAS) and Lukas Van Ness (6-5, 272, 4.58) — big, explosive, often under-productive in college but with elite athletic profiles and length. The preference is for size, power, and athletic upside over polished pass-rush technique. However, the 2025 draft showed some evolution: Green Bay drafted smaller, speed-oriented rushers (Collin Oliver at 6-1, 240) in later rounds. Under Hafley's 4-3 scheme, the 'LEO' weak-side DE needs to be a speed-to-power converter who can get after the QB.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-3 to 6-5 for premium picks (R1-R2). Smaller speed rushers possible on Day 3.
physical_profile.weight_range: 250-280 for premium picks. The Packers have historically drafted heavy EDGEs early.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33+ inches preferred — Gary had 34.25-inch arms, Van Ness had 33.75-inch arms
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: RAS (Relative Athletic Score) is the proxy. The Packers chase elite athleticism across the board. Vertical jump, broad jump, and 3-cone all matter. Gary had a 9.99 RAS. Van Ness had a 9.29 RAS. The Packers target near-perfect athletic profiles.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred. Speed is valued but size and explosion matter more than raw 40 time at EDGE.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Power and athletic upside. The Packers have historically drafted raw EDGEs with explosive first steps and high ceilings, not finished products with polished move repertoires.
most_valued_traits: Athletic versatility (can stand up or put hand in dirt)
least_important_traits: College sack production — the Packers have repeatedly drafted EDGEs with underwhelming college stats (Gary had 9.5 career sacks at Michigan, Van Ness had one productive season at Iowa)
deal_breakers: Undersized players without elite speed (in premium rounds)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite RAS / athletic testing combined with size. The Packers will reach for a physical freak with an underdeveloped pass-rush plan.

college_production.production_floor: No clear production floor for R1 picks — the Packers have drafted EDGEs with very modest college stats based on traits. Gary had just 9.5 career sacks. Van Ness had one big season.
college_production.production_trajectory: Context-adjusted. The Packers appear to value the quality of competition and role (rotational player at Georgia vs. full-time starter at a G5 school) over raw numbers.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — Gutekunst explicitly said of Gary: 'Production is just the way you look at it. He affected the passer, the game, and at times he wrecked it.' They look at pressures, disruption, and impact beyond sacks.

examples: GREEN BAY 2019 R1.12 — Rashan Gary: 6-4, 277, 4.58 40, 9.99 RAS. Only 9.5 career sacks at Michigan. Drafted for elite athletic profile and developmental ceiling.
GREEN BAY 2023 R1.13 — Lukas Van Ness: 6-5, 272, 4.58 40, 9.29 RAS. One productive season at Iowa. Drafted for size, versatility, and athleticism.
GREEN BAY 2025 R4 — Collin Oliver: 6-1, 240. Smaller speed rusher — represented an evolution in thinking, possibly influenced by Hafley's 4-3 needs.

EDGE.confidence_note: Based entirely on Packers lineage. Sullivan had no solo draft picks. The 2025 Packers draft showed some evolution toward smaller, speed-oriented rushers at EDGE, which may indicate Sullivan's own preferences versus the traditional Packers mold. Monitor the 2026 draft closely.

CB.archetype_summary: Long, physical, press-capable corner with size and speed. The Packers have consistently drafted tall corners: Jaire Alexander (5-10 but elite athleticism, R1 2018), Kevin King (6-1, R2 2017), Josh Jackson (6-0, R2 2018), Eric Stokes (6-1, 194, 4.25 40, R1 2021). Gutekunst cited 'the length we're looking for' and 'excellent size we look for' when discussing CB picks. Hafley's press-man, vision-based scheme demands corners who can play press at the LOS. The Packers/Hafley hybrid archetype is a long, fast, physical corner who can press in man and play disciplined zone with eyes on the QB.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31+ inches. Gutekunst specifically referenced Stokes' 32.5-inch arms as a positive trait.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is paramount: Stokes ran 4.25. The Packers have drafted fast corners with good size. 40-time and short-area quickness (3-cone, short shuttle) matter.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.45 or faster strongly preferred for outside CB.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Press-man capable with vision-based zone. Hafley's scheme plays a mix of Cover-1 and Cover-3, demanding corners who can press at the line but also play true zone with eyes on the QB. Not a pure man-coverage-only scheme.
most_valued_traits: Tackling — Hafley's defense requires CBs to support the run
least_important_traits: Pure zone instincts without press ability — scheme demands press-first corners
deal_breakers: Lack of speed (4.50+ is likely an eliminator)
Short arms/small frame for an outside corner role
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite length + speed combination. A 6-1+ corner with 4.35 speed and long arms would be very attractive to this front office.

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor. The Packers have not shown a preference for high-interception corners over physical, disruptive ones.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers multi-year starters from Power 5 programs based on Packers history.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values quality of opposition faced

examples: GREEN BAY 2018 R1.18 — Jaire Alexander: 5-10, 196, 4.38 40. Elite athleticism and ball skills. Became a shutdown corner.
GREEN BAY 2021 R1.29 — Eric Stokes: 6-1, 194, 4.25 40, 32.5-inch arms. Gutekunst cited 'excellent size we look for.'
GREEN BAY 2017 R2.33 — Kevin King: 6-1, 200, 4.43. Length and size prototype.

CB.confidence_note: Packers lineage provides strong signal. Hafley's scheme preferences add specificity — he needs press-man corners. CB is identified as a top need for Miami in 2026.

QB.archetype_summary: The Packers have a unique QB philosophy: draft QBs constantly, even when you have a franchise starter. Sullivan explicitly brought this philosophy to Miami: 'The quarterback position is the most important position in sports. We're going to invest in that position every year if we can.' The Packers drafted Aaron Rodgers behind Brett Favre, Jordan Love behind Aaron Rodgers, and Malik Willis as a Day 3 developmental pick. Sullivan confirmed at the combine that Miami WILL draft a QB in 2026. Given the roster situation (Tua Tagovailoa likely departing, Quinn Ewers as a 7th-round pick), QB is the most pressing need. Sullivan will not force a QB at the top if the value isn't there: 'We're not going to do it in an irresponsible manner where we sacrifice building the infrastructure of this football team so that when we do find our guy, he can go be successful.' The archetype based on Packers history: high-ceiling, potentially raw QB with arm talent and athletic ability. Love was a small-school QB with declining stats who fell in the draft. Rodgers was a controversial pick behind a future HOFer. Both had elite arm talent.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-1 to 6-5. Love is 6-4, Rodgers is 6-2. No strict height requirement but prefers taller QBs.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm velocity and mobility matter more than combine testing. The Packers value QBs who can extend plays. Love ran 4.74 and had a 35-inch vertical.

playing_style.primary_style: Athletic pocket passer who can extend plays. Not a run-first QB. The Packers have never drafted a pure running QB in the premium rounds.
most_valued_traits: Developmental ceiling — willingness to sit and learn
least_important_traits: Perfect college stats — Love's stats declined dramatically in his final college season and the Packers still traded up for him
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite arm talent with high developmental ceiling — the Packers have shown willingness to trade UP for a QB they believe in (Love trade in 2020)

college_production.production_floor: No clear floor — Love threw 17 INTs in his final college season. The Packers will look past poor stats if the talent and upside are there.
college_production.production_trajectory: Not production-dependent. Context matters more than numbers.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — Love's declining stats were attributed to poor supporting cast and scheme change at Utah State. The Packers look through the stats to the traits.

examples: GREEN BAY 2020 R1.26 — Jordan Love: 6-4, 224, 4.74 40. Traded UP from #30 to #26 to select. Sat 3 years behind Rodgers. Had 17 INTs in final college season.
GREEN BAY 2022 R3.86 — Malik Willis: Drafted by Jon Robinson in Tennessee, then traded to GB. Athletic, dual-threat QB with developmental profile.
Sullivan QUOTE: 'I've learned, if you can help it, don't wait till you don't have a quarterback to find one.'

QB.confidence_note: Sullivan's QB philosophy is well-documented from his own statements and the Packers' track record. He has confirmed Miami will draft a QB in 2026. The question is WHERE — if Sullivan doesn't see a franchise QB at #11, he may take one later and focus on building the supporting cast first.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile offensive tackle who can pass protect in a zone-based scheme. The Packers have valued athleticism and versatility in their OL picks: Elgton Jenkins started at 4 different positions, Zach Tom played tackle and guard, Jordan Morgan (R1, 2024) was a 311-pound athletic tackle. The 2025 draft showed a potential evolution toward bigger bodies (Anthony Belton at 336 lbs), but the traditional Packers OT prototype is an athletic mover who can execute zone runs and pass protect with technique over pure power. Bobby Slowik's Shanahan-tree offense will demand linemen who can move in space.

physical_profile.weight_range: 305-330 (traditional Packers preference around 310-315, recent 2025 picks trended heavier)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Athleticism is critical. The Packers want linemen who can pull, reach-block, and move in space. 40-time, 3-cone, and short shuttle matter for OTs in a zone scheme.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard cutoff but athletic testing is heavily weighted

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Zone-scheme fit: lateral mobility, footwork, ability to reach-block on outside zone runs. Must be capable pass protectors but don't need to be pure anchors.
most_valued_traits: Versatility (ability to play multiple OL positions)
least_important_traits: Pure power/anchor without mobility — the Packers don't want phone-booth maulers who can't move
deal_breakers: Poor athletic testing combined with limited pass protection
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athleticism with multi-position versatility. Jenkins was a 2nd-round pick who became a Pro Bowler because of his ability to play anywhere.

college_production.context_sensitivity: Conference strength and competition faced matters

examples: GREEN BAY 2019 R2.44 — Elgton Jenkins: 6-5, 311. Played 4 positions. Elite versatility. Pro Bowl selection.
GREEN BAY 2024 R1.25 — Jordan Morgan: 6-5, 311. Athletic tackle with zone-scheme fit.
GREEN BAY 2025 R2 — Anthony Belton: 6-6, 336. Bigger than typical Packers OT, representing possible evolution.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile interior lineman who can play multiple positions. The Packers want IOL who can pull, reach-block, and fit a zone-run scheme. Gutekunst said he wanted to 'build a bigger, physical, intimidating line' when discussing center Josh Myers. Recent additions trended bigger (Aaron Banks at 325, Anthony Belton at 336). The Packers value centers and guards who can move in space and pass protect.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard cutoff but prefers length
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short shuttle and 3-cone for interior movement. Bench press for power baseline.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Zone-scheme fit with ability to pull and get to the second level
most_valued_traits: Physicality at the point of attack
deal_breakers: Stiff, non-athletic linemen who can only play one position
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Ability to play center AND guard. Versatility is premium.

examples: GREEN BAY 2021 R2.62 — Josh Myers: 6-5, 310. Center from Ohio State. Gutekunst cited size.
GREEN BAY 2022 R3.92 — Sean Rhyan: 6-5, 321. Versatile guard/tackle.
GREEN BAY 2022 R4 — Zach Tom: Started at multiple OL positions as a rookie.

DT.archetype_summary: Penetrating, upfield defensive tackle in a 4-3 scheme. Hafley's defense moved the Packers from a 2-gap 3-4 to a penetrating 4-3. DTs in this scheme are asked to 'just go' rather than read-and-react. The prototype is a one-gap penetrator: Kenny Clark (6-3, 314, R1 2016) is the gold standard — he posted 10 sacks in 2023 playing an upfield role. Devonte Wyatt (6-3, 304, R1 2022) was another athletic, penetrating DT. Both had elite athletic profiles with size. Hafley's 4-3 asks the 3-technique to attack the B-gap and disrupt, not hold ground.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness (3-cone, short shuttle) is paramount. The Packers want explosive interior rushers.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Upfield penetration and disruption. One-gap, get-off-the-ball-and-attack scheme.
most_valued_traits: Ability to penetrate gaps and disrupt
least_important_traits: Two-gap ability — Hafley's scheme doesn't ask DTs to hold ground in space
deal_breakers: Slow-footed, heavy-bodied run-stuffers without pass-rush ability
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior pass-rush ability combined with size. Clark-type profile.

college_production.production_floor: Production matters more here than at EDGE — Clark had 5.5 sacks and 8 TFL in his final year at UCLA

examples: GREEN BAY 2016 R1.27 — Kenny Clark: 6-3, 314, 5.04 40. 3x Pro Bowler. Penetrating DT.
GREEN BAY 2022 R1.28 — Devonte Wyatt: 6-3, 304, 4.77 40. Athletic interior rusher from Georgia.

WR.archetype_summary: Big-bodied, physical receiver with contested-catch ability and speed. The Packers under Gutekunst have typically drafted WRs in rounds 2-3, not round 1 (2025 was the first R1 WR since 2002). They value size and speed: Christian Watson (6-4, 208, 4.36, R2 2022) is the prototype — tall, explosive, fast with ball-tracking ability. Dontayvion Wicks and Grant DuBose were also bigger receivers. The 2025 pick of Matthew Golden (4.29 speed from Texas) represented a new willingness to draft a WR in R1 for speed. Sullivan's stated philosophy of building 'inside out' suggests WR may not be the first priority, but Miami needs size at the position after losing Tyreek Hill.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is king. The Packers love explosive speed at WR. Watson ran 4.36, Golden ran 4.29. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosion proxies.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.45 or faster strongly preferred. The Packers covet game-breaking speed.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Speed and size combination. Ability to win downfield and at the catch point.
least_important_traits: Polished route running as the sole attribute — the Packers will take raw speed/size over a polished route-runner without burst
deal_breakers: Slow, undersized receivers without a clear NFL role
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: 4.3 speed combined with 6-2+ frame. Watson profile.

college_production.production_floor: No strict floor. Watson had moderate production at NDSU (a lower level). The Packers value traits over raw stats at WR.
college_production.production_trajectory: Will take late risers or developmental targets
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for scheme, role, supporting cast

examples: GREEN BAY 2022 R2.34 — Christian Watson: 6-4, 208, 4.36. NDSU product with elite athleticism.
GREEN BAY 2025 R1.23 — Matthew Golden: 4.29 speed, Texas. First R1 WR since 2002.
GREEN BAY 2025 R3 — Savion Williams: Another speed/size receiver.

TE.archetype_summary: Receiving TE with athleticism and versatility. The Packers have historically underinvested in TE relative to other positions but have found success with athletic TEs: Tucker Kraft (R3, 2023) has become a productive receiving TE. The scheme values TEs who can flex out, create mismatches, and contribute in the pass game while also blocking. Miami needs a TE badly after trading Jonnu Smith.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and vertical jump. The Packers want athletic TEs who can create mismatches.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster for a receiving TE

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Receiving ability and versatility — can line up inline, in the slot, or flexed out
least_important_traits: Pure blocking TE without receiving upside in early rounds
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite receiving ability with TE size

college_production.production_floor: Production-dependent at TE more than other positions
college_production.production_trajectory: TEs are traditionally late developers; patience is expected

examples: GREEN BAY 2023 R3.78 — Tucker Kraft: 6-5, 254. Athletic receiving TE from South Dakota State. Led NFL in YAC for TEs.

S.archetype_summary: Range safety for single-high role ('post safety' in Hafley's terminology) or hybrid safety who can play in the box and slot. Hafley's defense plays a lot of single-high safety looks (Cover 1 and Cover 3), demanding a center-fielder with elite range. The Packers invested heavily in safety in 2024: Xavier McKinney (free agent signing), Javon Bullard (R2), Evan Williams (R4). The Dolphins may look to trade Minkah Fitzpatrick, creating need at safety.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Range indicators: 40-time, vertical jump, broad jump. Must be able to cover ground as a single-high safety.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster for a deep safety. Slot/hybrid safety can be slightly slower with better short-area quickness.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Single-high center-field safety (Cover 1/Cover 3) or hybrid safety in the slot/box
most_valued_traits: Versatility (can play deep or drop into box)
least_important_traits: Pure box-safety run-stuffing without coverage ability
deal_breakers: Limited range for the deep safety role
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite ball production (picks) combined with range

examples: GREEN BAY 2024 R2.36 — Javon Bullard: Hybrid S/slot. Athletic and versatile.
GREEN BAY 2024 R4 — Evan Williams: Free safety with range. Became a starter.
GREEN BAY 2024 FA — Xavier McKinney: Free agent signing — elite single-high safety to anchor Hafley's scheme.

LB.archetype_summary: Athletic, three-down LB with coverage ability. Hafley's 4-3 demands two off-ball LBs (MIKE and WILL) who can run sideline-to-sideline, cover in space, and blitz. The Packers drafted Quay Walker (R1, 2022) — a 6-4, 241, 4.52 LB with elite RAS but limited production at Georgia. They also found Edgerrin Cooper (R3, 2024) — an immediate impact player. The archetype is a big, fast, athletic LB who may be raw but has elite physical tools.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: RAS is critical. Walker had a 9.63 RAS. The Packers want elite athletes at LB. 40-time, vertical jump, and 3-cone all matter.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Sideline-to-sideline speed and coverage ability in a 4-3 scheme
least_important_traits: College production — Walker's underwhelming stats at Georgia didn't concern the Packers
deal_breakers: Slow, stiff LBs who can't cover in space

college_production.production_floor: No clear floor — the Packers have drafted LBs with modest stats
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — Gutekunst didn't worry about Walker's production at Georgia because he was part of an elite defense

examples: GREEN BAY 2022 R1.22 — Quay Walker: 6-4, 241, 4.52 40, 9.63 RAS. Athletic freak from Georgia.
GREEN BAY 2024 R3.75 — Edgerrin Cooper: 75+ tackles, 13+ TFL, 3+ sacks, INT, FF as a rookie.

RB.archetype_summary: Day 2/Day 3 value pick — not a premium position. The Packers have found excellent RBs in mid-to-late rounds: Aaron Jones (R5, 2017), AJ Dillon (R2, 2020), Eddie Lacy (R2, 2013). They have never used a R1 pick on a RB under Thompson/Gutekunst. Sullivan is unlikely to prioritize RB early given De'Von Achane, Jaylen Wright, and Ollie Gordon II are already on the roster. If they draft one, expect a late-round power/complementary back.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and power combination. Jones was 5-9, 208 with 4.56 speed. Dillon was 6-0, 247 — a bruiser.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Complementary piece — value extraction from mid-to-late rounds
most_valued_traits: Power or speed as a complementary trait to the lead back
least_important_traits: Premium pedigree — the Packers believe in finding RB value late
deal_breakers: Using a R1 pick on a RB
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Unlikely to reach for any RB. Will take if value falls.

examples: GREEN BAY 2017 R5.182 — Aaron Jones: 5-9, 208. Led NFL in rushing TDs (16) in 2019.
GREEN BAY 2020 R2.62 — AJ Dillon: 6-0, 247. Power back complement to Jones.

reach_tolerance.notes: No personal draft data. The Packers under Gutekunst have made notable reaches: Rashan Gary was considered a reach by many analysts (consensus mid-teens, taken 12th), Quay Walker at 22 was considered a reach, Van Ness at 13 was questioned. The Packers are comfortable reaching 8-15 spots for players they believe in, especially at premium positions with elite athletic profiles. Sullivan is expected to carry this willingness. The reach tolerance of ~10 picks reflects the Packers' historical pattern of valuing their own board over consensus.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Shanahan-tree outside zone scheme under OC Bobby Slowik (former Texans OC). West Coast concepts with zone-run principles. Values athletic, versatile offensive linemen who can pull and reach-block. Demands quick-hitting passing game with play-action and RPOs off zone-run looks. Slowik's Houston offense featured C.J. Stroud in a quick-release, rhythm passing system.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Hafley 4-3 base defense. Press-man/vision-based hybrid. Single-high safety looks (Cover 1 and Cover 3 heavy). Penetrating defensive line — one-gap, get-upfield-and-attack philosophy. Mix of man coverage and true zone with eyes on QB. Blitzes sparingly overall but aggressively on third-and-long. Demands: press-capable outside CBs with size/speed, penetrating 3-technique DT, two EDGE rushers who can get after the passer in a 4-man rush, range safety for single-high, athletic LBs who can cover.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: COMPLETE REGIME CHANGE. Previous offense under McDaniel was a different Shanahan-tree variant (speed-based, motion-heavy). Previous defense under various coordinators was different. Hafley's 4-3 is a wholesale defensive scheme change from anything Miami has run recently. The offensive scheme transition may be smoother given Slowik's Shanahan-tree roots, but the offensive identity will still shift significantly. All scheme-dependent personnel preferences should be weighted toward Hafley/Slowik preferences, not prior Miami coaches.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate risk tolerance inferred from Packers lineage. The Packers have historically been willing to take upside bets on raw athletes (Gary, Van Ness, Walker) in early rounds but balance this with safer picks elsewhere. Sullivan's stated emphasis on 'culture' and 'character' suggests he won't be reckless. However, his willingness to blow up the existing Miami roster (releasing Hill, Chubb, attempting to trade Tua and Fitzpatrick) shows comfort with bold, uncomfortable decisions. A rebuilding team with cap constraints may force more calculated risk-taking.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Low-to-moderate recency bias expected. Sullivan is taking over a 7-10 team with needs everywhere — there is no single 'last year's failure' to overcorrect for. He inherits a blank slate. The Packers historically have been disciplined about not overreacting to single-season failures. Sullivan's emphasis on process and long-term building suggests low recency bias.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Sullivan has shown ZERO sunk cost attachment to the prior regime's decisions. He has aggressively moved off players signed to large contracts by the previous GM (Hill, Chubb, attempting to trade Tua). He released players with significant dead money because 'we needed to get younger and cheaper.' This is a strong signal that he will not inherit another GM's mistakes — he'll cut bait and rebuild from scratch.
psychological_profile.combine_description: High combine weight inferred from Packers lineage. Gutekunst said 'bigger, faster, stronger is something I've always been taught.' The Packers consistently draft near-perfect RAS scores at premium positions. Sullivan has spent years as the Packers' NFS combine representative early in his career — he was literally the team's point person at the combine for several years. Testing data matters significantly in this front office, particularly at EDGE, LB, CB, and WR.
psychological_profile.character_description: High character emphasis. Sullivan repeatedly mentioned 'culture' as the most important element of his team-building philosophy. His exact words: 'When you draft and develop and you raise your own, they're bought in. When you fall into the trap of bringing a bunch of guys in from all over the place, there is no standard. A team full of mercenaries is a scary place to be.' The Packers have historically been high-character organizations. Jon Robinson's addition (Titans tree, Patriots tree) reinforces this — Robinson valued character heavily in Tennessee.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Sullivan has an extremely proactive QB philosophy inherited from the Thompson/Gutekunst Packers: 'If you can help it, don't wait till you don't have a quarterback to find one.' He confirmed Miami WILL draft a QB in 2026. He's also exploring FA (Malik Willis) and may carry multiple QBs through 2026 while building the supporting infrastructure. He's explicitly stated he won't sacrifice roster-building to force a QB: 'We're not going to do it in an irresponsible manner where we sacrifice building the infrastructure.' This suggests he may draft a QB in round 2-4 while using the #11 pick on a non-QB if the value isn't there.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Traits over production. The Packers have a documented pattern of drafting underproductive college players with elite athletic traits: Rashan Gary (low sacks), Quay Walker (modest stats at Georgia), Jordan Love (17 INTs in final season). Gutekunst explicitly dismissed production concerns about Gary. Sullivan is expected to continue this — he values what a player CAN do based on physical tools over what they DID do based on college stats.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No strict threshold — Watson was a small-school product. Golden was highly productive at Texas. Varies.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: Productive college backs preferred in mid-to-late rounds
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: No minimum sack threshold — Gary had 9.5 career sacks and was drafted 12th overall
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical threshold — values film over INT totals
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: No production floor — Walker was underprodutive relative to his athletic profile
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: Some production expected — Clark had 5.5 sacks in final season

college_production_preferences.notes: This is a traits-and-projection front office, not a production-and-analytics one. The Packers have consistently shown they will bet on the physical ceiling of a player over what he did in college. This is one of the strongest signals from the scouting tree. Sullivan is expected to trust his scouts' evaluations over box scores.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Soft preferences based on Packers lineage. Not absolute hard cutoffs but strong tendencies. The Packers will occasionally break pattern for exceptional film evaluations, but they heavily lean toward elite athletes.

size_preferences_by_position.LB: 230-250 for off-ball LB in 4-3. Prefers bigger LBs (Walker was 241).
size_preferences_by_position.OL: 310+ across the board, trending bigger (325+ in 2025 class)

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: RAS (Relative Athletic Score) is the dominant metric for the Packers' evaluation process. They chase near-perfect RAS scores at premium positions. Sullivan spent years as the team's combine representative — testing data is deeply embedded in his evaluation DNA. Gutekunst doesn't use RAS by name but has 'similar proprietary analytical metrics.' Vertical jump and broad jump are heavily weighted as explosiveness proxies.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Measurables serve as QUALIFIERS, not eliminators, in this system. A player with elite film and mediocre testing will get consideration. A player with elite testing and mediocre film gets even MORE consideration — the Packers bet on athleticism translating with proper development. The 2025 Packers draft (Golden with limited combine data) showed willingness to look past incomplete testing when tape warranted it.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions (OL, EDGE, DT, CB, QB) with elite athletic profiles and high ceilings. Sullivan's first draft will likely focus on building the foundation — he has said 'build from the inside out' repeatedly. Expect OL, DL, or CB at pick #11 unless a franchise QB they love is available. Sullivan has signaled strong willingness to trade BACK from #11 if the board breaks unfavorably, accumulating Day 2 capital.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: This is where Sullivan has signaled the most excitement. With 5 top-100 picks, he wants to 'stock the cupboard.' Expect a mix of BPA and need-filling. QB will likely be drafted somewhere in rounds 2-4 if not taken in round 1. The Packers have historically used Day 2 for high-impact starters: Jenkins (R2), Watson (R2), Dillon (R2), Bullard (R2), Cooper (R3).
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, special teams contributors, and position depth. The Packers have found excellent value on Day 3: Aaron Jones (R5), Karl Brooks (R6), Evan Williams (R4). Sullivan will likely draft a developmental QB here if one wasn't taken earlier. Expect at least one 'traits over production' swing on Day 3.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: The Packers have historically used comp picks normally — no distinct pattern of taking higher risks with comp picks. Sullivan may treat them as extra opportunities to build depth.

hit_rate_by_position.note: No personal draft history. Cannot calculate hit rates. The Packers' hit rate under Gutekunst has been mixed: strong at OL (Jenkins, Tom, Rhyan all became starters), inconsistent at EDGE (Gary was good before ACL, Van Ness has disappointed), excellent Day 3 value (Jones, Brooks, Williams), poor at CB (Stokes, King both underwhelmed), mixed at LB (Walker inconsistent, Cooper excellent).

blind_spots: Based on Packers lineage: EDGE development may be a concern — Gary and Van Ness represent two high investments that haven't fully paid off. CB evaluation has been inconsistent (Stokes, King). These represent potential blind spots Sullivan may carry.
strengths: Based on Packers lineage: OL evaluation is strong. Day 3 value extraction is excellent. QB identification (Love) has shown long-term payoff. Running back value (Jones in R5) is a strength. Safety evaluation improved significantly in 2024 (Williams, Bullard both hit).

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Conservative spender who supplements through targeted free agency. Sullivan explicitly stated: 'I've always been taught the draft is your lifeblood. You build through the draft. I think you supplement through free agency.' The Packers have historically been one of the least active teams in free agency, making only 3-4 splash signings in 20 years. Sullivan will be even more constrained by Miami's cap situation (~$23M over the cap entering the offseason). He has already begun clearing cap space by releasing Hill and Chubb. Free agency will be 'intentional and deliberate' — targeted signings of difference-makers, not wholesale roster turnover through FA.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When the Packers sign a veteran starter at a position, they still draft depth and development at that position. Example: They signed Xavier McKinney at safety in 2024 and STILL drafted Javon Bullard and Evan Williams at safety in the same draft. Sullivan will likely follow this pattern — using FA for immediate starters and the draft for long-term depth.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft first and foremost. Sullivan's stated philosophy is clear: 'Draft, develop, and retain our own. When you raise your own, people are vested, they're bought in.' He views free agency as supplementary. The Miami rebuild will be draft-driven, with FA used only when the cap allows and only for targeted, culture-fit additions. Sullivan has compared his approach to the Packers' model: Charles Woodson (FA), then draft the foundation around him.

notable_patterns: ZERO DRAFTS AS GM. All patterns below are inferred from the Packers scouting tree (2016-2025 Gutekunst era, where Sullivan was directly involved as Director of College Scouting, Co-Director of Player Personnel, and VP of Player Personnel).
Packers consistently drafted EDGE and DL in round 1 (Gary R1 2019, Van Ness R1 2023, Clark R1 2016, Wyatt R1 2022)
Packers have invested heavily in OL on Day 2 (Jenkins R2, Myers R2, Rhyan R3, Tom R4, Morgan R1 2024, Belton R2 2025)
Packers value elite athletic profiles (RAS) at premium positions — near-perfect scores common
Packers draft QBs proactively (Love behind Rodgers in R1 2020, Willis traded for in 2024)
Packers rarely trade up — they stay put or trade down
Packers have never drafted a RB in round 1 under Thompson/Gutekunst
Sullivan has signaled he wants to trade DOWN from #11 in 2026 to accumulate more picks

confidence_notes: FIRST-TIME GM WITH ZERO DRAFTS. This profile is built almost entirely on Sullivan's 22-year career in the Green Bay Packers scouting tree and his extensive public statements since being hired in January 2026. There is no personal draft history to analyze. The profile carries high confidence on Sullivan's STATED philosophy (it is extremely well-documented and consistent) but low confidence on his ACTUAL behavior, which has not been tested. Key uncertainties: (1) Will Sullivan's actual picks match the Packers mold, or will he develop his own identity? (2) How much influence will Hafley, Kyle Smith, Jon Robinson, and Champ Kelly have on picks — all bring different scouting trees. (3) The cap-constrained, rebuilding context in Miami is very different from the competitive Packers teams Sullivan helped build. His risk tolerance and need-filling behavior may differ from the Packers' when working with a bare roster. REFRESH AFTER 2026 DRAFT is critical — this profile will fundamentally change once we have actual picks to analyze.
